title: Brownian Motion
properties: Stationary independent increments; strong Markov; martingale.
Paths: continuous, nowhere differentiable, unbounded variation on every interval.
Invariances: scaling $(c^{-1/2} W_{ct})_t \overset{d}{=} W$; time inversion $(t W_{1/t})_t \overset{d}{=} W$; rotation.
Transition density = heat kernel $p_t(x,y) = (2\pi t)^{-d/2} e^{-|x-y|^2/2t}$; generator $\tfrac{1}{2}\Delta$.
motivation: Brownian motion is the mathematical idealisation of a completely erratic path: the scaling limit of a random walk whose steps become infinitely small and infinitely frequent. It is simultaneously the simplest nontrivial continuous-time martingale, the simplest diffusion, and the driving noise from which all Itô processes are built — which is why it appears in every corner of stochastic analysis.
definition: A $d$-dimensional Brownian motion on a filtered probability space $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, (\mathcal{F}_t), \mathbb{P})$ is a continuous adapted process $(W_t)_{t \ge 0}$ with $W_0 = 0$ such that for all $0 \le s \le t$: (i) the increment $W_t - W_s$ is independent of $\mathcal{F}_s$, and (ii) $W_t - W_s \sim \mathcal{N}(0, (t-s)I_d)$. Wiener's theorem guarantees such a process exists; its law on $C([0,\infty), \mathbb{R}^d)$ is the Wiener measure.
examples: The rescaled simple random walk $S_{\lfloor nt \rfloor}/\sqrt{n}$ converges in distribution to Brownian motion (Donsker's invariance principle) — the functional central limit theorem.
$\mathbb{E}[W_s W_t] = \min(s,t)$; increments over disjoint intervals are independent Gaussians.

Almost every Brownian path is continuous everywhere but differentiable nowhere, and has unbounded variation on every interval — which is why the Itô integral cannot be a pathwise Riemann–Stieltjes integral.
consequences: Quadratic variation: $[W]_t = t$ almost surely. This single identity is the source of the second-order term in Itô's formula.
Brownian motion is a martingale, a Markov process, and a Gaussian process at once; each viewpoint spawns its own theory.
Its transition density is the heat kernel, so $u(t,x) = \mathbb{E}[g(x+W_t)]$ solves the heat equation — the seed of the Feynman–Kac formula.
